instruments.title: NESREA e-waste regulations
instruments.body: The main domestic framework prohibits open burning and landfill disposal while requiring manufacturers and importers to participate in Extended Producer Responsibility.

instruments.title: Basel and Bamako commitments
instruments.body: Nigeria is part of the main international legal architecture governing hazardous waste flows, including bans and prior informed consent obligations.

instruments.title: Basel e-waste amendments enter into force
instruments.body: From January 1, 2025, all transboundary e-waste and scrap shipments fall under prior informed consent requirements.

instruments.title: NESREA cooperative-led model
instruments.body: The new model aims to integrate informal workers through structured cooperatives tied to governance, financing, compliance, and social protection support.

gaps.title: Law exists, field conditions do not change at scale
gaps.body: Unsafe dismantling and burning continue because collection channels, recycling infrastructure, and enforcement reach remain weak.

gaps.title: Formal operators lose feedstock to informal markets
gaps.body: Informal recyclers and scavengers outcompete registered operators on price, leaving formal systems under-supplied.

gaps.title: Import controls remain hard to enforce
gaps.body: Customs data does not cleanly distinguish new from used electronics, making it difficult to police dumping through second-hand trade.

gaps.title: Market power remains uneven
gaps.body: At sites like Alaba, middlemen can profit substantially while informal collectors face unsafe work and weak bargaining power.
